snippets.title: Multinomial NB on SMS spam (sklearn)
snippets.code:
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_20newsgroups
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

# Binary subset: alt.atheism vs soc.religion.christian
categories = ["alt.atheism", "soc.religion.christian"]
train = fetch_20newsgroups(subset="train", categories=categories, remove=("headers", "footers", "quotes"))
test = fetch_20newsgroups(subset="test", categories=categories, remove=("headers", "footers", "quotes"))

clf = Pipeline([
    ("vec", CountVectorizer(max_features=5000, stop_words="english")),
    ("nb", MultinomialNB(alpha=1.0)),  # Laplace smoothing
])
clf.fit(train.data, train.target)
print(classification_report(test.target, clf.predict(test.data)))
snippets.explanation: `CountVectorizer` produces word-count columns; `MultinomialNB(alpha=1.0)` applies Laplace smoothing so unseen words do not zero out a class score.

snippets.title: Bernoulli NB with binary bag-of-words
snippets.code:
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.naive_bayes import BernoulliNB
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline

clf = Pipeline([
    ("vec", CountVectorizer(binary=True, max_features=5000, stop_words="english")),
    ("nb", BernoulliNB(alpha=1.0, binarize=0.0)),
])

# X must be 0/1; binary=True ensures counts > 0 become 1
clf.fit(train.data, train.target)
preds = clf.predict(test.data)
snippets.explanation: `binary=True` converts term counts to presence flags. Bernoulli NB models both P(word present | class) and P(word absent | class).

snippets.title: Inspect top weighted tokens per class
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

vec = clf.named_steps["vec"]
nb = clf.named_steps["nb"]
feature_names = np.array(vec.get_feature_names_out())

for class_id, class_name in enumerate(train.target_names):
    log_probs = nb.feature_log_prob_[class_id]
    top = log_probs.argsort()[-8:][::-1]
    print(class_name, ":", ", ".join(feature_names[top]))
snippets.explanation: `feature_log_prob_` exposes learned log θ_{y,i}—a quick sanity check that class-specific words (e.g., religious terms) rank highest for the matching label.
